content.text: Format consistency means every example in a few-shot prompt uses identical delimiters, field names, ordering, and whitespace. The model learns the output format by pattern-matching across the examples — if two examples use different separators or field names, the model does not infer a consistent rule; it treats the prompt as a noisy signal and may reproduce either pattern or invent a third one. This is one of the most common and easily-fixed causes of unreliable few-shot outputs.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def ask(prompt: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=30,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n\n"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return repr(resp.content[0].text.strip())


QUERY = "The update fixed the login bug."

# Bad: inconsistent field names and delimiter styles
inconsistent = f"""Input: The app crashed on startup.
Label: Negative

Text: Works great!
Sentiment - Positive

Message: Delivery took too long.
Result = Neutral

Input: {QUERY}
Label:"""

print("Inconsistent:", ask(inconsistent))
# Model may output any format — 'Positive', 'Sentiment - Positive', 'Result = Positive', etc.


# Good: identical structure in every example
consistent = f"""Text: The app crashed on startup.
Label: Negative

Text: Works great!
Label: Positive

Text: Delivery took too long.
Label: Neutral

Text: {QUERY}
Label:"""

print("Consistent:  ", ask(consistent))
# Reliably outputs exactly one label word on the same line
Must be identical across all examples
Using "Label:" in example 1 and "Answer:" in example 2
Single blank line or "---" — pick one
Some examples separated by one line, others by two
Mixed case across examples; model reproduces the mixture

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


class FewShotPrompt:
    """
    Ensures format consistency by generating all examples
    from the same template.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        input_field: str = "Text",
        output_field: str = "Label",
        separator: str = "\n\n",
    ):
        self.input_field  = input_field
        self.output_field = output_field
        self.separator    = separator
        self._examples: list[tuple[str, str]] = []

    def add(self, text: str, label: str) -> "FewShotPrompt":
        self._examples.append((text, label))
        return self

    def _format_example(self, text: str, label: str = "") -> str:
        result = f"{self.input_field}: {text}\n{self.output_field}:"
        if label:
            result += f" {label}"
        return result

    def build(self, query: str) -> str:
        parts = [self._format_example(t, l) for t, l in self._examples]
        parts.append(self._format_example(query))  # no label — model fills it
        return self.separator.join(parts)


# All examples rendered through the same template — no inconsistency possible
builder = (
    FewShotPrompt(input_field="Text", output_field="Label", separator="\n\n")
    .add("Absolutely love this product!",    "Positive")
    .add("Broken on arrival — very upset.",  "Negative")
    .add("Does the job. Nothing more.",       "Neutral")
)

test_queries = [
    "Shipped fast, exactly as described.",
    "Instructions are missing from the box.",
    "Worst purchase of the year.",
]

for query in test_queries:
    prompt = builder.build(query)
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    label = resp.content[0].text.strip()
    print(f"{label:10s}  {query}")

Format consistency is the cheapest reliability improvement available in few-shot prompting: it costs nothing to fix and the gains are immediate. The practical approach is to generate all examples programmatically from a single template rather than writing them by hand — hand-written examples almost always have small inconsistencies (a missing space, a different capitalisation, an extra newline) that are invisible to a human reader but that the model detects and may reproduce. A template enforces consistency structurally, not by inspection.

Large language models complete prompts by predicting the next token based on statistical patterns learned during pre-training and by the pattern visible in the current context window. In few-shot prompting, the examples you provide act as a local training signal: the model extracts the implicit rules of the task from the formatting of those examples. When two examples use different field names or different separators, the model cannot determine which convention is authoritative. It hedges by sometimes following one, sometimes the other, and sometimes inventing a blend.

The practical consequence is that downstream parsing breaks. If you extract the model's label by splitting on the colon after the Label field, a response that uses a different field name will silently produce a wrong result. Production pipelines that rely on structured model outputs fail in ways that are hard to debug because the model's actual answer is often correct; only the wrapper format is wrong. Format inconsistency is therefore not an aesthetic problem but a reliability engineering problem.

Research by Min et al. (2022) on in-context learning found that the format of few-shot examples is a stronger predictor of output quality than the semantic correctness of the labels themselves. Swapping correct labels for random ones barely reduces accuracy on many tasks, but removing format structure dramatically degrades performance. The implication is clear: the model is learning the format first, the content second.
When the output format is identical across all examples, you can write a simple deterministic parser that works reliably across all responses.
Consistent templates reduce the variance introduced by minor wording differences between examples, making the model's behaviour less sensitive to the order or phrasing of individual shots.
A structural validation pass is straightforward when the expected format is fixed. Inconsistent prompts make schema validation nearly impossible without re-running the model.
Libraries like Instructor and Outlines constrain token sampling to produce only structurally valid outputs. Format consistency is the prerequisite for schema enforcement to work.
When format consistency is enforced programmatically, format bugs are eliminated as a class. Remaining errors are semantic, which are far easier to diagnose.
Format consistency does not fix all few-shot reliability problems. Several limitations remain:
If examples genuinely require variation to capture different output structures, a single template may not capture the diversity.
A programmatic FewShotPrompt builder must be updated whenever the task schema evolves.
Consistent formatting with clear field names and separators adds tokens compared to a terse, dense prompt.
Does not compensate for unrepresentative examples.
A consistently formatted set of examples that does not cover edge cases will produce wrong answers for those edge cases.
Models with strong format priors may override the template.
Very large instruction-tuned models have strong priors about common output formats. Combine templates with system-prompt instructions for robust behaviour.

Self-attention in the transformer allows each output token to attend to any previous token in the context. When the model generates the label field for the query, the attention pattern is heavily influenced by the corresponding label positions in the earlier examples. If a consistent label field appears after each input field in the few-shot block, the model learns a strong conditional: label tokens follow input-field tokens and precede the next separator.

Mechanistically, the format tokens (field-name colons, separators, whitespace) function as structural markers that help the model segment the prompt into input-output pairs. These markers are encoded in the key-value pairs of the attention mechanism. If prior examples used inconsistent markers, the match between the current query position and prior label positions is weaker and noisier, producing less reliable output.

Temperature interacts with format consistency. At temperature 0, the model is deterministic and picks the most probable next token. Inconsistency often manifests as the model copying the format of whichever example it attends to most strongly. At higher temperatures, inconsistency spreads across multiple output formats. Setting temperature to 0 for structured extraction is the correct default, but it does not remove the underlying ambiguity from inconsistent templates.
When to Use Format Consistency (and When Not To)

Format consistency is mandatory whenever the model's output will be parsed programmatically. This covers: classification, named entity extraction, structured data conversion, slot filling, key-value extraction, and sentiment scoring. Any pipeline step whose downstream consumer reads a field by name requires format consistency.

Format consistency is less critical for open-ended generation tasks where the output is consumed by a human reader: free-form summarisation, creative writing, explanation generation, brainstorming. In these settings, imposing a rigid template would harm quality without improving reliability.
For instruction-tuned models like Claude, combining a consistent few-shot template with a system prompt instruction and a stop sequence is more robust than relying on the template alone.
Define your schema: identify input field name, output field name, output vocabulary, and separator style.
Write a template class that renders every example through the same formatting function.
Never write examples by hand directly in the prompt string; always call the template's add() method.
Set temperature=0.0 and a stop_sequence of newline to prevent the model from generating beyond the expected output.
Parse outputs with a function that expects exactly the template's format and raises an exception on unexpected structure.
Add a retry loop that catches parse failures and logs the raw response for debugging.
Regression-test the prompt against a small labelled evaluation set after any template change.

A customer support platform processes 50,000 incoming tickets per day and needs to route each to the correct team. A naive approach writes few-shot examples ad-hoc, resulting in 12% mis-routes. Replacing hand-written examples with a template-based FewShotPrompt reduces mis-routes to 3% without any change to the model or the examples themselves.
Writing examples as a raw multiline string
Hard to audit; small inconsistencies are invisible
Generate every example through a template class
Mixing Text: and Input: across examples
Model attends to both field names and uses neither reliably
Pick one field name and enforce it structurally
Inconsistent separators (blank line vs dashes vs equals)
Model treats separator style as part of the task semantics
Use one separator type, always generated by the template
Model generates extra text after the label, breaking parsers
Model outputs mixed case; case-sensitive downstream logic fails
Normalise all labels to one case in the template
Define one FewShotPrompt builder per task type and import it across all callers.
After parsing the label, check that it belongs to the known vocabulary. Raise an exception on unexpected output.
Test against a held-out evaluation set after every prompt change.
Even a small 50-example set catches regressions invisible in manual spot-checks.
Long examples consume context window. If examples must be long, put the most recently added example last.
Use system prompts for additional format enforcement.
An explicit system instruction is a second layer of format control on top of the few-shot template.
Log raw model outputs alongside parsed results.
When a parse error occurs in production, the raw response tells you exactly what format the model used.

The token overhead of a consistent few-shot block scales linearly with the number of examples (k) and the average example length (L tokens): total tokens is approximately k times L plus query length. For k=5 examples of 30 tokens each, this adds 150 tokens.

For batch classification tasks, the Anthropic Message Batches API allows up to 10,000 requests per batch with a 50% cost reduction. The few-shot prompt is identical for every item in the batch, so it can be composed once and reused. Prompt caching on the shared prefix further reduces cost by up to 90%.

Format errors do not scale gracefully: a mis-routed ticket causes a support escalation that requires human time to resolve. The ROI of enforcing format consistency is therefore not just token efficiency but avoiding the much larger cost of format-driven downstream failures.
Accepts a list of examples and an ExamplePromptTemplate specifying the format. It enforces the same template for all examples.
DSPy treats few-shot prompts as optimisable programs. Its teleprompter optimisers automatically select and format examples; format consistency is enforced internally.
Constrains the Anthropic or OpenAI API to return Pydantic-validated JSON, eliminating format errors entirely by schema enforcement at the sampling level.
Constrains token sampling to a specified grammar or regular expression, making certain classes of format inconsistency physically impossible at the generation level.
When a consistent few-shot block is used across many requests, prompt caching stores the KV cache of the shared prefix, reducing TTFT and cost.
Tasks the model handles well from training
When output must be valid JSON or structured

Format consistency has a subtle security implication: the delimiters you choose can also appear in user-supplied input, creating a prompt injection risk. If a user's input contains text that mimics the format of your label field and is inserted literally into the prompt template, the model may parse the injected text as an instruction and override the intended output. This is a delimiter collision attack.

The most effective mitigation is to wrap user content in XML tags that the model is instructed to treat as raw data, and to strip or escape any token that matches your delimiter from user input. The XML-tag approach is more robust because the model has seen XML-tagged raw data in pre-training and interprets it correctly as a boundary.

Reliability at scale requires a retry policy. Parse failures should trigger a retry with a higher-specificity prompt, not a silent default. A typical policy: retry once with the same prompt, then retry with an explicit instruction appended, then fall back to a default label and alert. Logging every parse failure gives a continuous quality signal that detects format drift before it affects users.
Format consistency means all few-shot examples use the same field names, delimiters, ordering, capitalisation, and separators, making the model's format-learning unambiguous.
The model learns prompt structure primarily from format, not from label correctness; format errors are more damaging than minor semantic errors in examples.
Always generate examples programmatically through a template class; hand-written examples almost always contain invisible inconsistencies.
Set temperature=0.0 and a stop sequence after the expected output to prevent format drift in the completion.
Validate output structure and membership in the expected vocabulary; raise exceptions (not warnings) on parse failure.
Sanitise user-supplied input with XML tags or delimiter escaping to prevent prompt injection via field-name collisions.
For high-volume pipelines, combine consistent templates with Anthropic prompt caching and the Message Batches API to minimise cost.
